dataName: sargassum muticum japanese wireweed sargassum algae
name: Japanese Wireweed
description: Invasive brown algae that forms dense floating mats and attaches strongly to hard surfaces like hulls.
features: Can grow up to several meters long

name: Wakame
description: Marine algae listed as invasive in Europe. Grows rapidly on hulls and port infrastructure.

name: Star Ascidian
description: Colonial invertebrate that encrusts on hulls, creating gelatinous mats that smother other species.

name: Striped Barnacle
description: Warm-water crustacean. Causes huge hydrodynamic drag on ships and spreads rapidly.

dataName: rugulopteryx okamurae asian brown weed algae
name: Asian Brown Weed
description: A highly aggressive brown algae that covers massive areas of the rocky seabed. Transferred via anchors and fishing gear.
features: Attaches strongly to hard surfaces and ropes

dataName: asparagopsis armata harpoon weed algae red
name: Harpoon Weed
description: An aggressive red algae officially listed in European legislation. Travels entangled in hull structures and equipment.

name: Club Tunicate
description: A filter-feeding sea squirt that creates heavy fouling on hulls and docks, significantly increasing vessel drag.
features: Club or baseball bat shape with a stalk

dataName: didemnum vexillum carpet sea squirt tunicate
name: Carpet Sea Squirt
description: A highly invasive colonial tunicate that creates fleshy, mat-like colonies that smother native marine life.
features: Pale orange, cream, or off-white color
Looks like dripping wax or vomit

name: Chinese Mitten Crab
description: Invasive crustacean that travels hidden in crevices or in ballast water. Migrates between the sea and freshwater estuaries.

name: Soft-shell Clam
description: Bivalve mollusc frequently found in sediments that accumulate at the bottom of sea chests of ships stationary for a long time.

name: Asian Shore Crab
description: A highly invasive crab replacing native species in Northern Europe. Often transported in sea chests and ballast water.

name: Sea Walnut
description: A devastating comb jelly transported in ballast water. It caused the collapse of fisheries in the Black Sea.
features: Rows of cilia that diffract light (rainbow effect)

name: Atlantic Blue Crab
description: A voracious predator, originally from the Americas, now devastating bivalve populations in the Mediterranean.
features: Olive green carapace with sharp lateral spines

dataName: rapana venosa veined rapa whelk snail
name: Veined Rapa Whelk
description: A large predatory sea snail that feeds on native oysters and mussels. Larvae spread via ballast water.
features: Bright orange coloring on the inside of the shell opening

name: Striped Eel Catfish
description: The only marine fish on the EU list of invasive alien species. Highly venomous and dangerous to divers.
features: Eel-like body with four pairs of barbels around the mouth
Brown-black body with two white/yellow horizontal stripes

name: Asian Green Mussel
description: A large, fast-growing mussel that severely fouls hulls, sea chests, and intake pipes throughout the Caribbean, Gulf of Mexico, and South America.
features: Vivid green to dark green outer shell edge
Can grow up to 16 cm (6 inches) long
Forms extremely dense clusters on hard structures

name: European Green Crab
description: Listed as one of the world's 100 worst invasive species. It travels globally via ballast water and sea chests, devastating native bivalve populations along North and South American coasts.
features: Five distinct spines on the outside edge of each eye
Carapace is broader than it is long
Color ranges from dark green to brown or reddish patches

name: Zebra Mussel
description: A freshwater and estuarine bivalve introduced via ballast water. It causes billions of dollars in damage by clogging cooling systems and ship internal piping in North America.
features: Distinctive zigzag or striped black-and-white pattern
Typically very small (the size of a fingernail)

name: Orange Cup Coral
description: An invasive stony coral that heavily fouls ship hulls and oil rigs in the Gulf of Mexico and Brazil, outcompeting native sponges and corals.
features: Bright orange, red, or yellow polyps
Does not rely on sunlight (can grow in dark sea chests)
Extends bright yellow tentacles at night to feed

dataName: ciona intestinalis vase tunicate sea squirt
name: Vase Tunicate
description: A solitary sea squirt that creates massive biofouling weight on vessel hulls and aquaculture gear across both coasts of the Americas.
features: Yellow or green tint at the tips of its siphons
Grows densely packed together like tubes
Can squirt water when squeezed or removed

name: Skeleton Shrimp
description: A highly invasive amphipod that travels in secondary biofouling (living on algae that grows on hulls). Extremely prevalent in US and Canadian ports.
features: Looks like an underwater praying mantis or stick insect
Thread-like body, often red or brown
Clings to algae, ropes, and buoys in massive swarms
